comment_text.sn20.7:1.2: The Dasārahas were probably the people known in Sanskrit as the Daśārṇa of the western Vindhya mountains. | The mudiṅga is a double-headed drum known today as mridangam, which is still widely used in southern India. It is named for the paste (“mud”, today called the karanai) applied to one of the heads that gives it its distinctive snare-like tone (chapu). The skin is attached with a wooden rim tightened with leather straps. | Āṇaka is another name for what is said to be a large military drum, which might be named after āṇi in the sense of “tuning peg”, a feature of the modern mardala. However, in this case, the context requires that the āṇi be a plug for patching the shell, after which the drum was named “Plugger”.
comment_text.sn20.7:1.4: The pokkhara is the “drumskin”. The phalaka is the “wooden shell”, a single hollowed-out log from which the mridangam is constructed.

root_text.sn20.7:1.2: “Bhūtapubbaṁ, bhikkhave, dasārahānaṁ ānako nāma mudiṅgo ahosi.
root_text.sn20.7:1.3: Tassa dasārahā ānake ghaṭite aññaṁ āṇiṁ odahiṁsu.
root_text.sn20.7:1.4: Ahu kho so, bhikkhave, samayo yaṁ ānakassa mudiṅgassa porāṇaṁ pokkharaphalakaṁ antaradhāyi.
root_text.sn20.7:1.6: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, bhavissanti bhikkhū anāgatamaddhānaṁ, ye te suttantā tathāgatabhāsitā gambhīrā gambhīratthā lokuttarā suññatappaṭisaṁyuttā, tesu bhaññamānesu na sussūsissanti na sotaṁ odahissanti na aññā cittaṁ upaṭṭhāpessanti na ca te dhamme uggahetabbaṁ pariyāpuṇitabbaṁ maññissanti.
root_text.sn20.7:2.1: Ye pana te suttantā kavikatā kāveyyā cittakkharā cittabyañjanā bāhirakā sāvakabhāsitā, tesu bhaññamānesu sussūsissanti, sotaṁ odahissanti, aññā cittaṁ upaṭṭhāpessanti, te ca dhamme uggahetabbaṁ pariyāpuṇitabbaṁ maññissanti.
root_text.sn20.7:2.2: Evametesaṁ, bhikkhave, suttantānaṁ tathāgatabhāsitānaṁ gambhīrānaṁ gambhīratthānaṁ lokuttarānaṁ suññatappaṭisaṁyuttānaṁ antaradhānaṁ bhavissati.
root_text.sn20.7:2.4: ‘ye te suttantā tathāgatabhāsitā gambhīrā gambhīratthā lokuttarā suññatappaṭisaṁyuttā, tesu bhaññamānesu sussūsissāma, sotaṁ odahissāma, aññā cittaṁ upaṭṭhāpessāma, te ca dhamme uggahetabbaṁ pariyāpuṇitabbaṁ maññissāmā’ti.

translation_text.sn20.7:1.2: “Once upon a time, mendicants, the Dasārahas had a mridangam drum called the Plugger.
translation_text.sn20.7:1.3: When the Plugger cracked, the Dasārahas patched it with another plug.
translation_text.sn20.7:1.4: Eventually, there came a time when the original wooden shell for the drumskin disappeared
translation_text.sn20.7:1.5: and all that was left of the mridangam Plugger was a mass of plugs.
translation_text.sn20.7:1.6: In the same way, in a future time there will be mendicants who won’t want to listen when discourses spoken by the Realized One—deep, profound, transcendent, dealing with emptiness—are being recited. They won’t actively listen or try to understand, nor will they think those teachings are worth learning and memorizing.
translation_text.sn20.7:2.1: But when discourses composed by poets—poetry, with fancy words and phrases, composed by outsiders or spoken by disciples—are being recited they will want to listen. They’ll actively listen and try to understand, and they’ll think those teachings are worth learning and memorizing.
translation_text.sn20.7:2.2: And that is how the discourses spoken by the Realized One—deep, profound, transcendent, dealing with emptiness—will disappear.
translation_text.sn20.7:2.3: So you should train like this:
translation_text.sn20.7:2.4: ‘When discourses spoken by the Realized One—deep, profound, transcendent, dealing with emptiness—are being recited we will want to listen. We will actively listen and trying to understand, and we will think those teachings are worth learning and memorizing.’
